introHtml: TypeScript questions appear in most modern frontend and fullstack interviews. Interviewers test generics, conditional types, mapped types, and utility types — not just basic annotations. Understanding structural typing and how TypeScript interacts with JavaScript runtime behavior is the real differentiator.

topics.bodyHtml:
type vs interface

Featureinterfacetype
Declaration mergingYes (augmentation)No
Extends syntaxextends keyword& (intersection)
Computed propertiesNoYes
Union typesNoYes
Primitive aliasesNoYes
Best forObject shapes, public APIsUnions, aliases, complex types

any vs unknown vs never

let a: any    = "hello"; a.foo();    // no error — opts out of type checking
let u: unknown = "hello"; u.foo();   // Error — must narrow first
if (typeof u === "string") u.toUpperCase(); // OK after narrowing

// never: value that never exists
function fail(msg: string): never { throw new Error(msg); }
function assertExhaustive(x: never): never { throw new Error("Not exhaustive: " + x); }

// Exhaustiveness check with discriminated unions
type Shape = { kind: "circle"; radius: number } | { kind: "square"; side: number };
function area(s: Shape): number {
  switch (s.kind) {
    case "circle": return Math.PI * s.radius ** 2;
    case "square": return s.side ** 2;
    default: return assertExhaustive(s); // Error if new case added
  }
}

Generics

// Basic generic
function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }

// Constrained generic
function getLength<T extends { length: number }>(arg: T): number {
  return arg.length;
}

// Generic with default
interface Box<T = string> { value: T; }
const b: Box = { value: "hello" }; // T defaults to string

// Generic interface
interface Repository<T, ID = string> {
  findById(id: ID): Promise<T | null>;
  save(entity: T): Promise<T>;
  delete(id: ID): Promise<void>;
}

Utility Types

type User = { id: number; name: string; email: string; role: string; };

type PartialUser   = Partial<User>;                     // all optional
type RequiredUser  = Required<Partial<User>>;           // all required
type ReadonlyUser  = Readonly<User>;                    // all readonly
type UserPreview   = Pick<User, "id" | "name">;         // subset
type UserNoId      = Omit<User, "id">;                  // exclude
type StringOrNum   = Exclude<string | number | boolean, boolean>; // string | number
type OnlyStrNum    = Extract<string | number | boolean, string | number>;
type NotNull       = NonNullable<string | null | undefined>; // string
type Fn = (x: number, y: string) => boolean;
type Ret  = ReturnType<Fn>;       // boolean
type Args = Parameters<Fn>;       // [x: number, y: string]
type PromResult = Awaited<Promise<string>>; // string

Mapped Types

type Optional<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };
type Nullable<T> = { [K in keyof T]: T[K] | null };
type Mutable<T>  = { -readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K] }; // remove readonly
type ReadonlyPartial<T> = { readonly [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };

Conditional Types & infer

type IsArray<T> = T extends any[] ? true : false;
type Flatten<T> = T extends Array<infer Item> ? Item : T;
type UnwrapPromise<T> = T extends Promise<infer R> ? R : T;
type FirstArg<T extends (...args: any) => any> =
  T extends (first: infer F, ...rest: any) => any ? F : never;

Template Literal Types & satisfies

type EventName = "click" | "focus" | "blur";
type HandlerName = `on${Capitalize<EventName>}`; // "onClick" | "onFocus" | "onBlur"
type CSSProperties = `${string}-${string}`;

// satisfies: check type without widening
const config = { port: 3000, host: "localhost" } satisfies Record<string, string | number>;
config.port.toFixed(); // TS knows it's number (not widened to string|number)

// as const: infer narrowest type
const routes = ["home", "about", "contact"] as const;
type Route = typeof routes[number]; // "home" | "about" | "contact"

// Module augmentation
declare module "express" {
  interface Request { user?: User; correlationId?: string; }
}

Common gotchas

// Q: Why does narrowing not work inside callbacks?
let x: string | null = "hello";
setTimeout(() => {
  x.toUpperCase(); // Error: x might be null (could change before callback)
}, 0);
// Fix: capture value
const copy = x;
setTimeout(() => copy?.toUpperCase(), 0);

// Q: Structural typing — same structure = compatible
interface Cat { meow(): void; }
interface Dog { meow(): void; } // same shape
const cat: Cat = { meow() {} };
const dog: Dog = cat; // OK! TS uses structural (not nominal) typing

// Q: Type widening with const
let x = 3;        // type: number (widened)
const y = 3;      // type: 3 (literal, not widened)
const z = [1,2,3] as const; // type: readonly [1, 2, 3]

// Q: keyof typeof
const obj = { a: 1, b: 2, c: 3 };
type Keys = keyof typeof obj; // "a" | "b" | "c"
function getValue(o: typeof obj, key: Keys) { return o[key]; }

// Q: Conditional distribution
type ToArray<T> = T extends any ? T[] : never;
type Result = ToArray<string | number>; // string[] | number[] (distributes)
// vs:
type NoDistrib<T> = [T] extends [any] ? T[] : never;
type Result2 = NoDistrib<string | number>; // (string | number)[]

items.question: What is the difference between `interface` and `type`, and why do people overstate it?
items.answerHtml: Both can describe object shapes. Interfaces are extendable and mergeable, while type aliases are more flexible for unions, intersections, tuples, and mapped types. In most day-to-day object modeling either works, so the better answer is about choosing the clearer tool for the use case rather than treating one as universally superior.
items.flashcardBackHtml: Interfaces are great for object contracts; type aliases are more flexible for unions, intersections, and advanced compositions.

items.question: What problem do generics solve?
items.answerHtml: Generics let you write reusable logic while preserving type information. Instead of returning any or duplicating functions per type, you express a relationship between input and output types so the compiler can infer or enforce correct usage.
items.flashcardBackHtml: Generics preserve type information in reusable code instead of falling back to any.

items.question: Why is `any` dangerous, and when is it still acceptable?
items.answerHtml: any opts out of type checking and lets unsound assumptions spread through the program. It can be acceptable at boundaries when migrating legacy code or dealing with unknown input, but ideally it should be narrowed quickly into safer types such as unknown plus runtime validation.

items.question: When would you use `unknown` instead of `any`?
items.answerHtml: Use unknown when a value truly could be anything but you still want the compiler to force validation before use. That makes it ideal at API boundaries, JSON parsing, and untrusted input handling.

items.question: Why can a type assertion hide real bugs even though the code compiles?
items.answerHtml: A type assertion tells TypeScript to trust you rather than proving safety. If the runtime value does not actually match the asserted shape, the compiler goes silent and the bug moves to production. Assertions should be a last resort, not a replacement for modeling or validation.

keyTakeaways.title: ⚡ Key Takeaways
itemsHtml: Structural typing: two types are compatible if their shapes match, regardless of name — duck typing with static analysis
Generics + constraints (extends) make code reusable without sacrificing type safety
Conditional types (T extends U ? X : Y) and mapped types ({ [K in keyof T]: ... }) enable powerful type transformations
unknown is safer than any — you must narrow it before use; any opts out of all type checking
as const freezes types to literal values, enabling precise discriminated unions
Utility types (Partial, Pick, Omit, ReturnType) eliminate boilerplate type definitions
